ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Kerabu Timun; A00LC (mixed vegetable salad) used as closest defensible anchor.

allergens: may contain dried shrimp (crustacean shellfish) in some variants

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kerabu Timun is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small side-dish bowl of dressed cucumber salad served alongside a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Vegetables and Salads
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for cucumber-based vegetable salads and comparable Malay side dishes. Values for the full Kerabu Timun preparation (cucumber, onion, lime dressing, chili, lemongrass) are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for similar low-calorie vegetable salads. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cucumber, raw, #11206) used as secondary cross-check for core macros.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Cucumber and Low-Carbohydrate Vegetable Salads
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kerabu Timun. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous cucumber and raw vegetable salad entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cucumbers have a GI of approximately 15; the lime-sugar dressing adds minimal carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (15 × 8.5) / 100 = 1.3 (very low).

hypertension.guidance: Kerabu Timun contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit added salt in the dressing and benefit from the dish's natural potassium content (150 mg/100 g), which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.3 per serving, Kerabu Timun has a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided added sugar in the dressing is minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kerabu Timun is extremely low in total fat (0.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides potassium and vitamin C, making it a heart-friendly side dish; sodium should be monitored if salt is added liberally.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C and phytochemical content; nightshade sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish provides vitamin C (12 mg/100 g) and phytochemicals from onions and lemongrass that may support antioxidant status; individuals sensitive to nightshades should note that bird's eye chili may act as an inflammatory trigger in some cases.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kerabu Timun is a traditional Malay cucumber salad widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore as a light, refreshing side dish. It is prepared by slicing fresh cucumbers and onions thinly, then tossing them in a zesty dressing of lime juice, sugar, and salt. Finely chopped bird's eye chilies provide a sharp spicy heat, while aromatic ingredients such as lemongrass and bunga kantan (torch ginger flower) contribute a distinctive floral and citrus depth. Some regional variants incorporate ground dried shrimp (udang kering) for umami or toasted grated coconut (kerisik) for added texture. The dish requires no cooking and is assembled fresh, preserving the crisp texture of the cucumber. Nutritionally, Kerabu Timun is very low in calories (45 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.5 g), and provides moderate carbohydrates (8.5 g) primarily from natural sugars in the vegetables and a small amount of added sugar in the dressing. It is a good source of vitamin C (12 mg) and potassium (150 mg), and has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally halal, gluten-free, and vegan in its base form. It is typically served alongside rich or oily main courses such as grilled fish, rendang, or curry to cleanse the palate and provide textural contrast.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for comparable cucumber-based Malay vegetable salads and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central raw cucumber data. No exact Kerabu Timun entry exists in any single approved source; values represent a well-supported estimate for the standard preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database cucumber entries.
notes: Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based vegetable salad with no animal-derived ingredients in the base recipe; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in the standard preparation. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based dish with no animal fat in the base recipe. Saturated_fat_g (0.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.05 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cucumber and onion component data. Water_g (88.0 g) estimated from HPB and USDA cucumber composition data. Sodium of 180 mg/100 g reflects a preparation with moderate added salt; home preparations may vary. Variants containing dried shrimp will have higher sodium and protein values. Dietary flags reflect the base recipe without dried shrimp; variants with dried shrimp are not vegan or vegetarian. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for Kerabu Timun as a named dish.
